title: Longest Palindrome After Substring Concatenation II
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals for this challenge. All variants avoid the O(nm) DP table by scanning mirrored diagonal runs between s and reverse(t).

variants.languageReason: C++ is the strongest practical fit for the length-1000 string grid: native character loops, contiguous vectors, cheap reverse construction, and no interpreter or GC overhead. Center expansion has tiny constants at this bound, and the diagonal scan avoids the large DP table.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It matches LeetCode's string API, keeps all hot loops native and contiguous, and offers a clean low-memory implementation of the diagonal shell scan.
Considered for manual memory control, but the C API adds pointer and boundary boilerplate while not improving the O(n + m) auxiliary profile beyond the C++ memory variant.
Considered for native performance and safety, but bounds checks and UTF-8 indexing ergonomics add overhead for this fixed lowercase ASCII scan.
Considered for compiled loops, but slice bounds checks and runtime metadata are less attractive than C++ for repeated nested character comparisons.
Considered because JITs can handle O(nm) scans, but UTF-16 strings, object overhead, and runtime startup are unnecessary for n,m <= 1000.
Considered for implementation brevity, but interpreter/VM overhead in the O(nm) character grid is a poor match for the top 1% runtime target.

variants.approachJustification: Compute pure-palindrome middle lengths by center expansion, then scan each diagonal of s against reverse(t), combining the current equal shell length with the best center at the next boundary.

variants.languageReason: C++ keeps Manacher, heap-event sweeps, and the diagonal scan in low-overhead native code. This variant has the sharpest preprocessing bound while retaining compact linear storage.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Native vectors, priority queues, and inlined Manacher loops give the best balance of asymptotic preprocessing and judge-time constants.
Could implement the same idea manually, but custom heaps/events add risk and verbosity without a meaningful runtime advantage on LeetCode.
Native speed is viable, but heap/event plumbing and string indexing are less ergonomic for this ASCII-only LeetCode workload.
Compiled performance is acceptable, but bounds checks in the nested scan and heap operations make constants weaker than C++.
JIT performance can be competitive after warmup, but object-heavy event lists and runtime overhead are less predictable for top-percentile submissions.
The Manacher preprocessing would be linear, but the required O(nm) nested scan remains too slow relative to native submissions.

variants.approachJustification: Use Manacher to get all palindrome radii, sweep active centers to recover the longest palindrome starting at each boundary, and run the same DP-equivalent diagonal shell scan.

variants.languageReason: C++ fixed stack arrays and uint16_t lengths keep memory very close to a manual C layout while preserving LeetCode's straightforward string method signature.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Fixed arrays, direct indexing, and uint16_t storage minimize auxiliary memory without leaving the platform's idiomatic class interface.
Considered for bare pointers, but it would require a separate platform signature and more manual boilerplate while offering no asymptotic memory win.
Can be memory-tight, but stack-array ergonomics and UTF-8 indexing are more cumbersome for this LeetCode string problem.
Slices are simple but carry runtime metadata and bounds checks; they do not beat fixed C++ arrays for the memory objective.
Managed arrays add object headers and runtime overhead, so they are weaker for minimum reported memory.
High per-object and string representation overhead makes these unsuitable for the memory extreme.

variants.approachJustification: Store longest palindromes starting in s and ending in t in fixed uint16_t arrays, then scan anti-diagonals directly in the original grid without a reversed copy or DP table.

reasoningSummary: A cross-boundary palindrome consists of a mirrored shell where s matches reverse(t), followed by a pure palindromic center in one string. Consecutive matches on one diagonal encode the successful DP transitions from the hints, so scanning diagonal runs plus precomputed pure-palindrome centers gives the exact answer without storing dp[i][j].
timeComplexity: O(n^2 + m^2 + n * m) recommended/memory; O(n * m + (n + m) log(n + m)) runtime
